note.soap:
S: Patient reports a tick bite over 72 hours ago. No fever reported. Rash present. Location is Georgia. Patient is not pregnant.

O: Not applicable for phone encounter; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A:
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease given tick bite, rash >72 hours, and endemic area (Georgia).
  - Differential:
    1. Early Lyme disease (erythema migrans)
    2. Localized tick bite reaction
    3. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)
    4. Cellulitis or other skin infection
  - Red flags considered: No fever, no systemic symptoms reported. Rash present >72 hours warrants evaluation.

P:
  - Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended for physical examination and possible initiation of antibiotic therapy.
  - Plan: Advise patient to seek prompt in-person care to assess rash characteristics and consider Lyme disease treatment. Educate on signs of systemic illness (fever, joint pain, neurologic symptoms).
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to go to emergency department if fever, worsening rash, severe headache, neck stiffness, or neurological symptoms develop.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
